Beginners build a CRUD app, it works, and then they try to add one “small” feature: validation rules, friendly errors, sorting, pagination, roles, file upload, or even just one more screen. Suddenly the code that looked clean in a demo becomes fragile. This is where many beginners get stuck.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The code may work, but the design is already becoming hard to change.
This article is a practical tutorial for Angular + Spring Boot developers: not “how to make CRUD”, but what CRUD is really teaching you, and how to steer your project toward real-application structure.
The CRUD Is Not the Point: what CRUD is actually training you for
CRUD is useful because it forces you to connect the full stack:

• a UI that collects data

• an API that accepts requests

• persistence that saves and reads

• basic workflows like create, edit, list, delete
But real applications are not “four endpoints and a table”. Real applications are:

• rules (what is allowed, what is not)

• boundaries (who is allowed to call what)

• change (requirements…
